speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever a witness for war crimes trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did go to some. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father was really obsessed with these trials. Now, it wasn't that easy to-- to get tickets. But he always managed. And I went with him quite a number of times to keep him company. And the major war criminals were being tried there-- Szalasi and company-- the Arrow Cross. And on one occasion, my father got terribly excited. He was-- Szalazi was an arrogant [INAUDIBLE], you know. And my father couldn't contain himself. He started shouting in the courtroom. And we were unceremoniously bundled out. The police came, lifted up my hand-- my father hand and put him out. And-- but we were back next day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was the-- what was his crimes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Szalasi was the head of government. And they were trying to prove-- and it's very difficult to prove-- that these people were really murderous.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the result of that trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: They hanged them all-- most of them, I'd say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- were with these courts conducted by the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, they were conducted by the Hungarians, yeah. Quite a number of Jews were active. The-- quite often, they-- the prosecutor, as well as the defense, were Jewish lawyers. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you only viewed these. You were never a witness in any crime-- in any war crimes trials.

speaker: interviewee
text: But these were war crime trials.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I mean as in you weren't a witness for the prosecution.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my father was.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a witness for the prosecution.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but I wasn't there then. The man who had him taken away was caught. And he was responsible for the lives of hundreds of Jews. He knew a lot of-- he had a lot of Jewish friends, and he took over a private hospital, then encouraged these Jewish friends to go there and take all their valuables, jewelry, and whatnot. And when the place was full, he'd call his Gestapo friends and have it evacuated, split the loot. He was also hanged in the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in Austria, you mentioned to me that you were involved in some research.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it wasn't really because of any conviction. It was really a job that Simon Wiesenthal started an organization that exists to this day trying to document Nazi war crimes. And my job was in my spare time to go to the National Library and go through systematically all the wartime papers-- Viennese papers-- if I could find any Jewish references. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I did this for-- for quite a while. How much money it paid me, I can't remember. But it was handy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever meet Simon Wiesenthal?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't honestly remember. I must have met him. My immediate superior was a bloke called [? Tova ?] Friedman, who also went to Israel. And incidentally, he tracked down Eichmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any of the evidence? Did you research any of the evidence for Eichmann?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, I really can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you decide to come to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, look, I lived in the American zone of Vienna. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] for every refugee, every Jew, it was open to go to America, no problems at all. But somehow, America wasn't far enough for me. I wanted to-- I wanted to go as far from Europe as possible. And look, I didn't realize that at the time. But I must have been really badly hurt. I mean, when you consider I was brought up to be a patriotic Hungarian and surely that must be the ultimate rejection, that they pack up your people in cattle trucks and send them to the slaughter to Auschwitz. And I even considered New Zealand as being-- you can't go any further than New Zealand. But it wasn't easy to get to New Zealand, and I had an uncle living in Melbourne who barely got here by the skin of his teeth. So that's how I finished up here.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get papers to come to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle sent me a-- a landing permit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the date you arrived in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was May the 15th, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you arrived in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: What, immediately?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. For employment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I went to work for my uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the company?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a [INAUDIBLE] knitting mills in Camberwell.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1951, December.

speaker: interviewer
text: To-- what's the name of your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vivien.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we finish, I'd like to ask you an open question.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you put it into perspective your experiences during the Holocaust with your life today?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's no way-- no way-- you know, that you can put it in-- in perspective. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That would mean, wouldn't it, that there must be some connection? There is no connection. There can't be a connection. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was a different world. And this is a different world.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Ever since I came to Australia, and that's almost 50 years now, there's been no problems, you know? I've never been called a bloody Jew. I've never been called a bloody foreigner.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived here happily ever after. And you know, a friend of mine, almost every time we speak, we congratulate ourselves for-- for having come to Australia. I mean, even today, I mean, when you look at the town where I was born, 50 kilometers away people are killing each other in-- in Yugoslavia. Who needs that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But as to perspective, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, perhaps it has something to do with what I said before, that in this-- in the peaceful atmosphere of Australia-- let's say a normal atmosphere, what I would regard as normal among humans-- the-- the enormity of it all, of what happened there sinks in, that they were hunting people for nothing more than the blood coursing through their veins that the majority regarded as non-kosher. There was no other reason. I mean, you could say that there will be an edict tomorrow that every-- every person with brown eyes has to report for deportation to Devil's Island or whatever. There is no-- well, that is the perspective, I suppose. It's crazy.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I look back to Europe in-- in the war years, it's crazy today. How else could you look at it? That the effort and the-- the money and whatnot they expended in killing Jews-- that's unbelievable, incredible, today, that they would send my father with one guard-- one Jew, one guard-- all the way to Auschwitz. Can you-- can you believe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's-- it's almost a nearly sort of fairytale. It's a nightmare, really. It's the stuff that nightmares are made of.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, pleasure.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Robert. And I'm Andrew's first, youngest son. And here's Paul. He's the middle son, and David, who's the oldest son. And this is my wife, Linda, and my youngest, Ruby, my middle daughter, Alice, my oldest daughter, Hannah. Paul's wife, Margaret's absent at the moment. But he's got his three children here-- Kate, the eldest, Lee, the middle, and Mark, the youngest. And absent is also Paul's wife, Margaret. Who is in the factory. I think I mentioned that. And also David's wife Linda, who just had a baby that's four days old now, and her name is Cassandra. That's us, and of course my father's wife Vivien.

speaker: interviewer
text: Robert, could you briefly tell me how you and your brothers growing up related to your father's experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a pretty regular topic of conversation in the household. We-- I think all of us were aware of his experiences. We certainly heard a lot of stories. We heard a lot of stories that were factual and some are very human. And I-- I-- I'm sure that my brothers and I will remember many of the stories that were told both on a human level, some of which were inspiring and hopeful and some of which were just very sad, and-- and stories about relatives, many of whom were lost. I think it was always something we were aware of, that the immediate family survived-- something that to this day, I still think as being remarkable, given that there were so many families that there wasn't-- that there wasn't a complete unit that survived. And yeah, so that was something that came up in conversation a fair bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you feel it distinguished you in-- at and university? Did you feel different to your peers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. And I think that-- I couldn't speak for Paul and David on this. But I was always conscious of the fact going to a non-Jewish school, as we all did, that there was a difference there. And I think always very conscious, and to this day on very conscious of-- you know, racism has a-- it sort of really is a very strong feeling I ever have when somebody wants to attack Asians or anybody else in this country. It's sort of like bristles for me. And I-- and I think that my brothers feel the same way growing up in that environment, that it's a bristle on the back of the neck the moment, you know. Whether it's Asian or Greek or Italian, that sort of thing-- it registers very strongly. And I think that for a generation like us growing up with-- with-- with my father's influence, you'd have to feel the same way about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you very much. And thank you all for coming in. Thank you once again, Mr. Furst.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Andrew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I don't quite know what you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is the boy you're talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: The third row from the front-- yes, from the front-- and second from the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what do you remember about this boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember much about him at school, but I do remember him during the German occupation as the driver of a flash car, carting Nazi top dogs. And I remember him giving the Hitler salute.

speaker: interviewer
text: What school was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a place called [HUNGARIAN] in Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This would have been taken in 1948, I would think. It was my mother's idea that this'll be the last opportunity to take as a family. So we went to a photographer and had it taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your parents stay in Budapest until they died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, at that tome, they were in Budapest. But they came to Melbourne in 1956. My brother went to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the First World War, I would think, probably 1915, '16.

speaker: interviewer
text: What uniform is your father wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the uniform of the Austro-Hungarian army.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his rank?

speaker: interviewee
text: He got as far as corporal, but on this one he's just a private.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe this photo for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, obviously, it was taken on the day of my parents' wedding. It was taken in a little village called [HUNGARIAN], where my mother's foster parents lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Andrew, could you tell me who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's myself when I was 9 or 10 years old. And at that time, I used to conduct the kids' services in the synagogue and always in a sailor uniform, as it was the fashion then. And my mother got the idea to have this taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Nagykanizsa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Andrew, could you describe this documents to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's my matriculation certificate. And it's obviously a government document with a coat of arms on it. And I notice it was signed by some government commissioners, but it's also signed by the headmaster, Dr. Raphael [? Fuchs ?] and several of the teachers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Jewish high school of Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year did you receive this certificate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Must have been in '46. The date must be on it, but I just can't-- must have been in '46, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Andrew, could you tell me about the document on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my student pass from Vienna-- Technical University in Vienna. And the right is a membership card of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of Society of Jewish University Students in Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you receive this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must have been in 1946. When I started at the-- at the University there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Andrew could you describe the document on the top for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can translate it. It says it issued by the committee-- the Organization for the Victims of National Socialism in Austria, and the students only. And this one has obviously been issued for foreigners. But I really can't remember for what purpose that was issued anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you get these documents.

speaker: interviewee
text: All in-- that one in 1946, I would think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Andrew can you tell me what this document is?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the travel document that I used to come to Australia. The Hungarians revoked my passport and citizenship when I decided to come to Australia. And I had to get a travel document from the Austrians.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Andrew can you tell me what this document is?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my birth certificate. And that was issued in 1946. I imagine I would have needed it for my passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it issued?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was issued in Nagykanizsa, and interesting that the religion still shows on it, even after the war.
